refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability of ≥0.97 (97%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the system is available for productive operation to total scheduled operational time, accounting for planned maintenance, unplanned downtime, and safety-driven shutdowns.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-004 is a key performance indicator (KPI) that directly impacts customer satisfaction and mission success. Refining it requires explicit definition of: (1) the measurement period and baseline, (2) what constitutes 'available' vs. 'unavailable' (e.g., does safety-driven shutdown count as unavailability?), (3) the scope of the system (vehicle only, or vehicle + teleoperation + support services), and (4) the role of planned vs. unplanned downtime. High safety criticality means availability must not be achieved by deferring safety actions.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime events) across the evaluation period; cross-reference with maintenance logs, safety event records, and customer operational schedules; calculate availability as (total_scheduled_time - total_downtime) / total_scheduled_time; compare against 0.97 threshold and customer-defined target.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The Vehicle subsystem shall maintain mechanical and electrical readiness with mean time between failures (MTBF) sufficient to support ≥0.97 availability.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The Teleoperation subsystem shall maintain network connectivity and command/response latency within operational limits to prevent availability loss due to communication failure.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The Safety Services subsystem shall detect and respond to hazardous conditions without introducing unnecessary downtime, balancing safety and availability.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE Solution shall provide real-time availability monitoring, predictive maintenance alerts, and rapid fault recovery mechanisms to minimize unplanned downtime.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant critical subsystems (dual motors, dual communication links, dual safety controllers) with automatic failover to eliminate single points of failure.
DP-B: Implement predictive maintenance and condition-based monitoring with scheduled preventive maintenance intervals optimized to minimize unplanned downtime while maintaining safety margins.

DP-C: Implement a hierarchical availability management strategy: (1) design for high MTBF through component selection and thermal/stress management, (2) design for rapid MTTR through modular architecture and hot-swap capability, (3) implement real-time health monitoring and automated recovery protocols.

DP-D: Implement a service-level agreement (SLA) framework with contractual availability targets, performance tracking, and escalation procedures; rely on operational discipline and customer coordination rather than technical redundancy.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Implement a hierarchical availability management strategy combining high MTBF design (component selection, thermal management, stress analysis), rapid MTTR design (modularity, hot-swap, automated recovery), and real-time health monitoring with automated failover protocols.

potential_sources: DP-C couples availability to Vehicle subsystem design (MTBF), Teleoperation subsystem design (network resilience), and Safety Services subsystem design (health monitoring). This is acceptable because availability is a system-level property that inherently depends on all subsystems.
DP-C does not create unintended coupling between independent functional requirements; it is a coherent strategy that addresses the root causes of unavailability (failures and recovery time).
coupling_check.notes: DP-C is preferred over DP-A (redundancy alone is costly and may not address MTTR) and DP-D (SLA framework alone does not improve technical availability). DP-B (predictive maintenance) is complementary to DP-C and should be incorporated as a sub-strategy.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: High MTBF is achievable through component selection and design practices; risk: component suppliers may not meet MTBF targets, or field conditions may degrade MTBF below predictions.
Assumption: Automated recovery protocols can be designed to be safe and reliable; risk: recovery logic may introduce new failure modes or safety hazards if not rigorously validated.
Assumption: Real-time health monitoring can detect incipient failures before they cause unavailability; risk: sensor noise, false positives, or undetectable failure modes may limit effectiveness.

Assumption: Modularity and hot-swap capability can be implemented without compromising safety or performance; risk: hot-swap procedures may introduce transient safety hazards or require operator training.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-C should be refined to explicitly address the trade-off between availability and safety: automated recovery protocols must be designed such that they never bypass or defer safety-critical checks. Recommend adding a sub-strategy: 'Safety-aware recovery: all automated failover and recovery actions shall be validated against the safety case and shall not introduce new hazards.'

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution is the top-level system boundary that integrates Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems. Availability management is a system-level concern that spans all subsystems and requires coordination across Vehicle health monitoring, Teleoperation network resilience, and Safety Services hazard detection and response.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Real-time health and status data (MTBF indicators, fault codes, recovery events, availability metrics) transmitted from Vehicle and Safety Services to external monitoring systems (customer dashboard, maintenance systems).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operational commands and maintenance scheduling directives from Site Manager and Maintenance/Quality stakeholders; includes planned downtime windows and availability target updates.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety-critical alerts and shutdown notifications from Safety Services; availability management must respect safety-driven shutdowns and not attempt to override them.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communication backbone supporting Teleoperation and health monitoring; availability of Network Link directly impacts Teleoperation subsystem availability and real-time health monitoring capability.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the correct boundary for availability management because: (1) availability is a system-level KPI that requires coordination across all subsystems, (2) the DP (hierarchical availability strategy) spans Vehicle design, Teleoperation resilience, and Safety Services monitoring, (3) all external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are system-level concerns. Minimal diffusion: availability monitoring logic is centralized in NGHE Solution; subsystems contribute health data via Telemetry interface.

Vehicle subsystem owns MTBF design and mechanical/electrical readiness (FR-NGHE-004.1), but does not own Teleoperation resilience (FR-NGHE-004.2) or Safety Services integration (FR-NGHE-004.3). Assigning availability management to Vehicle alone would diffuse responsibility for network resilience and safety integration across multiple subsystems.

Teleoperation subsystem owns network connectivity and latency management (FR-NGHE-004.2), but does not own Vehicle MTBF or Safety Services integration. Assigning availability management to Teleoperation alone would create high diffusion and miss critical Vehicle and Safety Services contributions.

Safety Services subsystem owns hazard detection and response (FR-NGHE-004.3), but does not own Vehicle MTBF or Teleoperation resilience. Assigning availability management to Safety Services alone would diffuse responsibility and create coupling between safety and availability optimization that may conflict.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Availability management is a system-level orchestration concern that requires: (1) real-time aggregation of health data from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, (2) coordination of recovery actions across subsystems, (3) enforcement of safety constraints on recovery logic, and (4) reporting of availability metrics to external stakeholders. This orchestration is best owned by NGHE Solution as the integrating system boundary. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services contribute to availability through their respective sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-004.1, 004.2, 004.3) but do not own the system-level availability KPI.
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution availability management may become a 'god module' that knows too much about internal subsystem details; mitigation: define clear, abstracted health monitoring interfaces (Telemetry) that subsystems expose without revealing implementation details.

Risk: Availability management logic may conflict with safety-driven shutdowns if not carefully designed; mitigation: Safety Services owns SafetyAlert interface and has veto power over recovery actions; NGHE Solution must respect all SafetyAlert signals.

Risk: Real-time health monitoring and automated recovery may introduce latency or computational overhead that impacts Vehicle or Teleoperation performance; mitigation: design health monitoring as a low-priority background task with bounded resource consumption.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine the NGHE Solution IM boundary to explicitly define: (1) the availability monitoring and orchestration module (e.g., 'Availability Manager' component within NGHE Solution), (2) the health data aggregation interface (Telemetry), (3) the safety constraint enforcement mechanism (SafetyAlert veto), and (4) the automated recovery action protocols (with safety validation gates). This will clarify ownership and reduce the risk of diffusion.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (hierarchical availability management) directly addresses FR-NGHE-004 by combining high MTBF design, rapid MTTR design, and real-time health monitoring. The DP is logically sound and does not introduce unintended coupling. The DP is implementable and measurable (MTBF and MTTR are standard metrics). Recommended: add explicit safety constraint to DP-C to ensure recovery actions do not bypass safety checks.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk 1: Health monitoring logic may be scattered across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems if not centrally coordinated; mitigation: NGHE Solution owns the Availability Manager component that aggregates Telemetry data and orchestrates recovery actions.

Diffusion Risk 2: Automated recovery protocols may be implemented independently in each subsystem, leading to inconsistent behavior; mitigation: NGHE Solution defines recovery action standards and validates all recovery logic against safety constraints.

Diffusion Risk 3: MTBF and MTTR targets may not be clearly allocated to subsystems; mitigation: FR-NGHE-004.1, 004.2, 004.3 allocate specific availability contributions to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the correct IM boundary because it owns the system-level availability orchestration and can enforce consistency across subsystems. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are contained within NGHE Solution and contribute to availability through their respective sub-FRs. The Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, and Network Link interfaces provide clear boundaries for subsystem interaction.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Availability can be verified by collecting operational telemetry (uptime/downtime events) and calculating the ratio of available time to scheduled time. This requires: (1) instrumentation of Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services to report health status and downtime events via Telemetry interface, (2) integration with customer operational schedules and maintenance logs, (3) automated calculation of availability metrics. Verification is feasible and aligns with standard industry practices.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR-NGHE-004 is allocated to NGHE Solution as the system-level KPI. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-004.1, 004.2, 004.3, 004.4) are allocated to Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services, and NGHE Solution respectively. Allocation is clear and traceable. Recommended: define specific MTBF and MTTR targets for each subsystem to support availability allocation.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Refine DP-C to explicitly include safety constraint enforcement: 'All automated recovery actions shall be validated against the safety case and shall not introduce new hazards.'
Revision 2: Define the Availability Manager component within NGHE Solution IM boundary to clarify ownership of health monitoring and orchestration logic.
Revision 3: Allocate specific MTBF and MTTR targets to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems to support availability decomposition and verification.
Revision 4: Define the Telemetry interface schema to specify health data fields (fault codes, recovery events, availability metrics) that subsystems must report.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-004 is a system-level KPI that requires decomposition into subsystem-level FRs to be implementable. The refined FR and selected DP are sound, and the NGHE Solution IM boundary is correct. However, the sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-004.1, 004.2, 004.3, 004.4) require further decomposition to define specific design parameters and implementation modules for Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services, and NGHE Solution. Proceeding to the next decomposition level will clarify ownership, reduce diffusion risk, and enable detailed design and verification planning.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The Vehicle subsystem shall maintain mechanical and electrical readiness with mean time between failures (MTBF) sufficient to support ≥0.97 availability.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The Teleoperation subsystem shall maintain network connectivity and command/response latency within operational limits to prevent availability loss due to communication failure.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The Safety Services subsystem shall detect and respond to hazardous conditions without introducing unnecessary downtime, balancing safety and availability.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE Solution shall provide real-time availability monitoring, predictive maintenance alerts, and rapid fault recovery mechanisms to minimize unplanned downtime.

assumptions: Assumption 1: Availability is measured as (total_scheduled_time - total_downtime) / total_scheduled_time, where downtime includes both planned maintenance and unplanned failures, but excludes safety-driven shutdowns (which are treated as operational constraints, not availability failures).
Assumption 2: High MTBF is achievable through component selection, thermal management, and stress analysis; field MTBF may differ from design predictions.
Assumption 3: Automated recovery protocols can be designed to be safe and reliable; recovery logic must be validated against the safety case.
Assumption 4: Real-time health monitoring can detect incipient failures with acceptable false positive rate; sensor noise and undetectable failure modes may limit effectiveness.
Assumption 5: Modularity and hot-swap capability can be implemented without compromising safety or performance; hot-swap procedures require operator training and safety validation.
Assumption 6: Customer-defined availability target is ≥0.97 unless otherwise specified; target may be adjusted based on operational experience and cost-benefit analysis.
open_risks: Risk 1: Component suppliers may not meet MTBF targets; mitigation: conduct supplier qualification and field validation testing.
Risk 2: Automated recovery protocols may introduce new failure modes or safety hazards; mitigation: rigorous validation against safety case and operational testing.
Risk 3: Real-time health monitoring may have high false positive rate, leading to unnecessary downtime; mitigation: tune monitoring thresholds based on field data and operator feedback.
Risk 4: Network Link availability may be a bottleneck for Teleoperation subsystem; mitigation: design redundant communication paths and fallback modes.

Risk 5: Safety Services may conflict with availability optimization (e.g., conservative safety margins may reduce availability); mitigation: balance safety and availability through risk-based design and stakeholder agreement.

Risk 6: Availability measurement may be disputed if downtime attribution (planned vs. unplanned, safety-driven vs. failure-driven) is ambiguous; mitigation: define clear downtime categories and logging procedures.
evidence_needed: Evidence 1: Component MTBF data from suppliers and field validation testing.
Evidence 2: Teleoperation network reliability analysis and redundancy design documentation.
Evidence 3: Safety case validation of automated recovery protocols.
Evidence 4: Operational telemetry data (uptime/downtime events) from pilot deployments or similar systems.
Evidence 5: Customer operational schedule and maintenance plan.
Evidence 6: Availability measurement methodology and downtime attribution procedures.
Evidence 7: Predictive maintenance algorithm validation and false positive rate analysis.
Evidence 8: Hot-swap procedure safety validation and operator training materials.
